speaker: KING
text: Let fame, that all hunt after in their lives,

speaker: KING
text: Live registered upon our brazen tombs,

speaker: KING
text: And then grace us in the disgrace of death,

speaker: KING
text: When, spite of cormorant devouring time,

speaker: KING
text: Th’ endeavor of this present breath may buy

speaker: KING
text: That honor which shall bate his scythe’s keen edge

speaker: KING
text: And make us heirs of all eternity.

speaker: KING
text: Therefore, brave conquerors, for so you are

speaker: KING
text: That war against your own affections

speaker: KING
text: And the huge army of the world’s desires,

speaker: KING
text: Our late edict shall strongly stand in force.

speaker: KING
text: Navarre shall be the wonder of the world;

speaker: KING
text: Our court shall be a little academe,

speaker: KING
text: Still and contemplative in living art.

speaker: KING
text: You three, Berowne, Dumaine, and Longaville,

speaker: KING
text: Have sworn for three years’ term to live with me,

speaker: KING
text: My fellow scholars, and to keep those statutes

speaker: KING
text: That are recorded in this schedule here.

speaker: KING
text: Your oaths are passed, and now subscribe your names,

speaker: KING
text: That his own hand may strike his honor down

speaker: KING
text: That violates the smallest branch herein.

speaker: KING
text: If you are armed to do as sworn to do,

speaker: KING
text: Subscribe to your deep oaths, and keep it too.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: I am resolved. ’Tis but a three years’ fast.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: The mind shall banquet though the body pine.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Fat paunches have lean pates, and dainty bits

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Make rich the ribs but bankrout quite the wits.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: My loving lord, Dumaine is mortified.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: The grosser manner of these world’s delights

speaker: DUMAINE
text: He throws upon the gross world’s baser slaves.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: To love, to wealth, to pomp I pine and die,

speaker: DUMAINE
text: With all these living in philosophy.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I can but say their protestation over.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: So much, dear liege, I have already sworn,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That is, to live and study here three years.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But there are other strict observances:

speaker: BEROWNE
text: As not to see a woman in that term,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Which I hope well is not enrollèd there;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And one day in a week to touch no food,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And but one meal on every day besides,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: The which I hope is not enrollèd there;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And then to sleep but three hours in the night,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And not be seen to wink of all the day—

speaker: BEROWNE
text: When I was wont to think no harm all night,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And make a dark night too of half the day—

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Which I hope well is not enrollèd there.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: O, these are barren tasks, too hard to keep,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Not to see ladies, study, fast, not sleep.

speaker: KING
text: Your oath is passed to pass away from these.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Let me say no, my liege, an if you please.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I only swore to study with your Grace

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And stay here in your court for three years’ space.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: You swore to that, Berowne, and to the rest.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: By yea and nay, sir. Then I swore in jest.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: What is the end of study, let me know?

speaker: KING
text: Why, that to know which else we should not know.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Things hid and barred, you mean, from common sense.

speaker: KING
text: Ay, that is study’s godlike recompense.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Come on, then, I will swear to study so,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To know the thing I am forbid to know:

speaker: BEROWNE
text: As thus—to study where I well may dine,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: When I to feast expressly am forbid;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or study where to meet some mistress fine

speaker: BEROWNE
text: When mistresses from common sense are hid;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or having sworn too hard-a-keeping oath,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Study to break it, and not break my troth.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: If study’s gain be thus, and this be so,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Study knows that which yet it doth not know.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Swear me to this, and I will ne’er say no.

speaker: KING
text: These be the stops that hinder study quite,

speaker: KING
text: And train our intellects to vain delight.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Why, all delights are vain, and that most vain

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Which with pain purchased doth inherit pain:

speaker: BEROWNE
text: As painfully to pore upon a book

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To seek the light of truth, while truth the while

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Doth falsely blind the eyesight of his look.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Light seeking light doth light of light beguile.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: So, ere you find where light in darkness lies,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Your light grows dark by losing of your eyes.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Study me how to please the eye indeed

speaker: BEROWNE
text: By fixing it upon a fairer eye,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Who dazzling so, that eye shall be his heed

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And give him light that it was blinded by.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Study is like the heaven’s glorious sun,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That will not be deep-searched with saucy looks.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Small have continual plodders ever won,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Save base authority from others’ books.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: These earthly godfathers of heaven’s lights,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That give a name to every fixèd star,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Have no more profit of their shining nights

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Than those that walk and wot not what they are.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Too much to know is to know naught but fame,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And every godfather can give a name.

speaker: KING
text: How well he’s read to reason against reading.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: Proceeded well, to stop all good proceeding.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: He weeds the corn, and still lets grow the weeding.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: The spring is near when green geese are a-breeding.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: How follows that?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Fit in his place and time.

speaker: DUMAINE
text: In reason nothing.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Something then in rhyme.

speaker: KING
text: Berowne is like an envious sneaping frost

speaker: KING
text: That bites the firstborn infants of the spring.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Well, say I am. Why should proud summer boast

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Before the birds have any cause to sing?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Why should I joy in any abortive birth?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: At Christmas I no more desire a rose

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Than wish a snow in May’s new-fangled shows,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But like of each thing that in season grows.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: So you, to study now it is too late,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Climb o’er the house to unlock the little gate.

speaker: KING
text: Well, sit you out. Go home, Berowne. Adieu.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: No, my good lord, I have sworn to stay with you.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And though I have for barbarism spoke more

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Than for that angel knowledge you can say,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Yet, confident, I’ll keep what I have sworn

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And bide the penance of each three years’ day.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Give me the paper. Let me read the same,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And to the strictest decrees I’ll write my name.

speaker: KING
text: How well this yielding rescues thee from shame.

speaker: BEROWNE
text:
Item

                .
               Hath this been proclaimed?

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Four days ago.

speaker: BEROWNE
text:
Let’s see the penalty.

              Who devised this penalty?

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Marry, that did I.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Sweet lord, and why?

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: To fright them hence with that dread penalty.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A dangerous law against gentility.

text:

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This article, my liege, yourself must break,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For well you know here comes in embassy

speaker: BEROWNE
text: The French king’s daughter with yourself to speak—

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A maid of grace and complete majesty—

speaker: BEROWNE
text: About surrender up of Aquitaine

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To her decrepit, sick, and bedrid father.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Therefore this article is made in vain,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Or vainly comes th’ admirèd princess hither.

speaker: KING
text: What say you, lords? Why, this was quite forgot.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: So study evermore is overshot.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: While it doth study to have what it would,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: It doth forget to do the thing it should.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And when it hath the thing it hunteth most,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: ’Tis won as towns with fire—so won, so lost.

speaker: KING
text: We must of force dispense with this decree.

speaker: KING
text: She must lie here on mere necessity.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Necessity will make us all forsworn

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Three thousand times within this three years’ space;

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For every man with his affects is born,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Not by might mastered, but by special grace.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: If I break faith, this word shall speak for me:

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I am forsworn on mere necessity.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: So to the laws at large I write my name,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And he that breaks them in the least degree

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Stands in attainder of eternal shame.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Suggestions are to other as to me,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But I believe, although I seem so loath,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I am the last that will last keep his oath.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But is there no quick recreation granted?

speaker: KING
text: Ay, that there is. Our court, you know, is haunted

speaker: KING
text: With a refinèd traveler of Spain,

speaker: KING
text: A man in all the world’s new fashion planted,

speaker: KING
text: That hath a mint of phrases in his brain;

speaker: KING
text: One who the music of his own vain tongue

speaker: KING
text: Doth ravish like enchanting harmony,

speaker: KING
text: A man of compliments, whom right and wrong

speaker: KING
text: Have chose as umpire of their mutiny.

speaker: KING
text: This child of fancy, that Armado hight,

speaker: KING
text: For interim to our studies shall relate

speaker: KING
text: In high-born words the worth of many a knight

speaker: KING
text: From tawny Spain lost in the world’s debate.

speaker: KING
text: How you delight, my lords, I know not, I,

speaker: KING
text: But I protest I love to hear him lie,

speaker: KING
text: And I will use him for my minstrelsy.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Armado is a most illustrious wight,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A man of fire-new words, fashion’s own knight.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: Costard the swain and he shall be our sport,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: And so to study three years is but short.

speaker: DULL
text: Which is the Duke’s own person?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This, fellow. What wouldst?

speaker: DULL
text: I myself reprehend his own person, for I am his

speaker: DULL
text: Grace’s farborough. But I would see his own

speaker: DULL
text: person in flesh and blood.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This is he.

speaker: DULL
text: Signior Arm-, Arm-, commends you.

speaker: DULL
text: There’s villainy abroad. This letter will tell you

speaker: DULL
text: more.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Sir, the contempts thereof are as touching

speaker: COSTARD
text: me.

speaker: KING
text: A letter from the magnificent Armado.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: How low soever the matter, I hope in God

speaker: BEROWNE
text: for high words.

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: A high hope for a low heaven. God grant

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: us patience!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To hear, or forbear hearing?

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: To hear meekly, sir, and to laugh moderately,

speaker: LONGAVILLE
text: or to forbear both.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Well, sir, be it as the style shall give us cause

speaker: BEROWNE
text: to climb in the merriness.

speaker: COSTARD
text: The matter is to me, sir, as concerning

speaker: COSTARD
text: Jaquenetta. The manner of it is, I was taken with

speaker: COSTARD
text: the manner.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: In what manner?

speaker: COSTARD
text: In manner and form following, sir, all those

speaker: COSTARD
text: three. I was seen with her in the manor house,

speaker: COSTARD
text: sitting with her upon the form, and taken following

speaker: COSTARD
text: her into the park, which, put together, is “in manner

speaker: COSTARD
text: and form following.” Now, sir, for the manner.

speaker: COSTARD
text: It is the manner of a man to speak to a woman. For

speaker: COSTARD
text: the form—in some form.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: For the “following,” sir?

speaker: COSTARD
text: As it shall follow in my correction, and God

speaker: COSTARD
text: defend the right.

speaker: KING
text: Will you hear this letter with attention?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: As we would hear an oracle.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Such is the sinplicity of man to hearken after

speaker: COSTARD
text: the flesh.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This is not so well as I looked for, but the

speaker: BEROWNE
text: best that ever I heard.

speaker: KING
text:
Ay, the best, for the worst.
                 
                To
                 
                Costard
                .
               But,

speaker: KING
text: sirrah, what say you to this?

speaker: COSTARD
text: Sir, I confess the wench.

speaker: KING
text: Did you hear the proclamation?

speaker: COSTARD
text: I do confess much of the hearing it, but little

speaker: COSTARD
text: of the marking of it.

speaker: KING
text: It was proclaimed a year’s imprisonment to be

speaker: KING
text: taken with a wench.

speaker: COSTARD
text: I was taken with none, sir. I was taken with a

speaker: COSTARD
text: damsel.

speaker: KING
text: Well, it was proclaimed “damsel.”

speaker: COSTARD
text: This was no damsel neither, sir. She was a

speaker: COSTARD
text: virgin.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: It is so varied too, for it was proclaimed

speaker: BEROWNE
text: “virgin.”

speaker: COSTARD
text: If it were, I deny her virginity. I was taken

speaker: COSTARD
text: with a maid.

speaker: KING
text: This “maid” will not serve your turn, sir.

speaker: COSTARD
text: This maid will serve my turn, sir.

speaker: KING
text: Sir, I will pronounce your sentence: you shall

speaker: KING
text: fast a week with bran and water.

speaker: COSTARD
text: I had rather pray a month with mutton and

speaker: COSTARD
text: porridge.

speaker: KING
text: And Don Armado shall be your keeper.

speaker: KING
text: My Lord Berowne, see him delivered o’er,

speaker: KING
text: And go we, lords, to put in practice that

speaker: KING
text: Which each to other hath so strongly sworn.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: I’ll lay my head to any goodman’s hat,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: These oaths and laws will prove an idle scorn.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Sirrah, come on.

speaker: COSTARD
text: I suffer for the truth, sir; for true it is I was

speaker: COSTARD
text: taken with Jaquenetta, and Jaquenetta is a true

speaker: COSTARD
text: girl. And therefore welcome the sour cup of prosperity.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Affliction may one day smile again, and till

speaker: COSTARD
text: then, sit thee down, sorrow.
